sections.paragraph:
μαθηματικός, ή, όν (s. prec. entry; the adj. in various senses: ‘fond of learning, mathematical, astronomical’: Pla. et al.; SibOr 13, 67), subst. μαθηματικός, οῦ, ὁ (Aristot. et al). The study of mathematics was a core feature of ancient learning and was closely associated with study of celestial phenomena. The latter emphasis in our lit. and in the sense astrologer (M. Ant. 4, 48; Sext. Emp., Adv. Math.; Philo, Mut. Nom. 71) D 3:4.—DELG s.v. μανθάνω.

prec. prec. = preceding

adj. adj. = adjective

Pla Pla , V–IV b.c.; s. also Ps.-Plato—List 5

et et al. = et alii (and others)

SibOr SibOr = Oracula Sibyllina, II–III a.d.—List 5

subst. subst. = substantive(ly)

Aristot Aristot , various works, IV b.c.—List 5

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

  1. Ant. M. Ant. = Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, II a.d.—List 5

Sext. Emp. Sext. Emp. = Sextus Empiricus, II–III a.d.—List 5

Math Math = mathematician

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

D D = Didache, except that in a list of manuscripts or as textual variant D refers to Codex Bezae—List 1

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 609
